coredata.dc:title: Nano-optosensor based on titanium dioxide and graphene quantum dots composited with specific polymer for cefazolin detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Chansud, Nutnicha
bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: An optosensor using nanocomposite probes was fabricated for the detection of trace cefazolin. The nanoprobes utilized the high affinity of titanium dioxide, the good optical properties of graphene quantum dots and the good selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymer. The integration of these materials produced a rapid, highly sensitive optosensor with excellent selectivity for cefazolin detection. The fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite probes was quenched when cefazolin re-bound with the imprinted recognition cavities of the nanoprobes. The fabricated nanoprobe exhibited a good linearity for cefazolin from 0.10 to 10.0 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.10 μg L−1. The imprinting factor of the nanoprobe was 10.6 and selectivity for cefazolin was not affected by the analogue structures of cephalexin, cefatriaxone, cephradine, cefaperazone and ceftazidime. This nano-optosensor probe successfully detected cefazolin in milk and recoveries were between 85.0 and 97.4 % with RSDs less than 5.0 %. The results of analysis with nano-optosensor were in good agreement with HPLC analysis. The fabrication strategy of the nanocomposite probe can be modified for the measurement of other toxic compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Titanium dioxide
Cefazolin
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Optosensor
Fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample pretreatment and real sample analysis
Effect of mole ratio of template (TP) to monomer (MN) and cross linker (CL)
Sensitivity of different probes toward detection of cefazolin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of GQDs and GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes for cefazolin detection.
A TEM image of GQDs (A), an SEM image of GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes (B), size distribution histograms of GQDs (C) and GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes (D) (100 particles measured for each sample).
Effect of incubation time (A), pH of buffer (B) and TiO2 loading on the sensitivity of the detection of cefazolin using the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe.

The effect of the mole ratio of template to monomer (A) and template to cross linker (B) on the sensitivity toward cefazolin of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes. The experimental conditions were GQDs-TiO2-MIP dispersed in citrate-phosphate buffer solution at pH 6, incubation time of 15 min and amount of TiO2 of 50 mg.
Sensitivity for the detection of cefazolin using different nanocomposite fluorescence probes; sensitivity was obtained with the concentration of cefazolin in the range of 0.10-10.0 μg L−1.
Selectivity of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe for the detection of cefazolin and a competitive binding study (inset).
Fluorescence spectra of the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe (A), GQDs-TiO2-NIP probe (B) and a photograph of GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe in the absence (left) and presence of cefazolin (right) (C).
HPLC chromatograms of milk samples spiked with different cefazolin concentrations (B); 0.05 (a), 0.10 (b), 0.50 (c) and 1.0 mg L−1 (d).
The analysis of cefazolin in milk using nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe.
Comparison of analytical methods for the detection of cefazolin.

head.ce:title: Nano-optosensor based on titanium dioxide and graphene quantum dots composited with specific polymer for cefazolin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optosensor using nanocomposite probes was fabricated for the detection of trace cefazolin. The nanoprobes utilized the high affinity of titanium dioxide, the good optical properties of graphene quantum dots and the good selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymer. The integration of these materials produced a rapid, highly sensitive optosensor with excellent selectivity for cefazolin detection. The fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite probes was quenched when cefazolin re-bound with the imprinted recognition cavities of the nanoprobes. The fabricated nanoprobe exhibited a good linearity for cefazolin from 0.10 to 10.0 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.10 μg L−1. The imprinting factor of the nanoprobe was 10.6 and selectivity for cefazolin was not affected by the analogue structures of cephalexin, cefatriaxone, cephradine, cefaperazone and ceftazidime. This nano-optosensor probe successfully detected cefazolin in milk and recoveries were between 85.0 and 97.4 % with RSDs less than 5.0 %. The results of analysis with nano-optosensor were in good agreement with HPLC analysis. The fabrication strategy of the nanocomposite probe can be modified for the measurement of other toxic compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Cefazolin is a β-lactam antibiotic. It is effective against Gram-positive bacteria and is commonly administered to livestock to prevent disease or promote growth [1]. However, the overuse or misuse of cefazolin leaves residues in animal food products such as meat and milk. Residual cefazolin can affect humans, causing stomach pain, diarrhea, vomiting and fainting [2]. Moreover, it can increase bacterial resistance, with significant consequences for human health. Therefore, the determination of residual cefazolin in animal food products is a necessary precaution.

The methods employed for the detection of cefazolin have included chemiluminescence [3], capillary zone electrophoresis [4], Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometry [5], and spectrophotometric [6] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2,7]. Although these techniques successfully determine cefazolin, sensitivity can be low and some techniques are complicated and require expensive instruments. An interesting alternative method is spectrofluorimetry. This method is simple, rapid and convenient for cefazolin detection. The analytical performance of this technique can be easily improved using a composite probe [8]. Quantum dots have been effectively utilized as optical probes for the determination of target analytes such as copper ion [9,10], and salicylic acid [11]. The types of quantum dots used for optosensing applications have included semiconductor quantum dots (QD) and carbon-based quantum dots. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have also attracted attention for sensing applications owing to their low toxicity and good optical properties [12]. To improve their selectivity for the detection of organic compounds such as salbutamol [13], aflatoxins [14], cephalexin [15] and amoxicillin [16], QD fluorescent probes have been composited with high specificity materials of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIP can be easily synthesized in the presence of a target analyte which provides the template molecule, a functional monomer which can interact with the template molecule to produce specific binding sites and a cross-linker to form an imprinting polymer layer [17]. When the template molecule is removed, specific recognition cavities are left behind that can bind with target molecules during detection [18]. The fabrication of MIP using the target molecule as a template molecule produces highly selective fluorescent probes. Other advantages of MIP are good chemical stability, plasticity and cost-effectiveness. To improve binding between the target analyte and the fluorescent probe, a high affinity material can be included to produce a composite fluorescent probe. For cefazolin detection, titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles are a good high affinity material to include in a nanocomposite fluorescent probe. It contain hydroxyl group which can interact with cefazolin via hydrogen bonding [19,20]. In addition, TiO2 has been used as an adsorption material for the extraction of phenolic compounds [21], phthalate esters [22], phospholipids [23], N-nitrosamines [24] and polychlorinated biphenyls [25]. Therefore, it should improve the adsorption ability of a nanocomposite fluorescent probe.

In this work, an optosensing nanoprobe for the detection of cefazolin was developed for the first time. The nanoprobe was fabricated by integrating the high affinity of TiO2 nanoparticle, the good optical properties of GQDs and the high specificity of MIP. The combination of these materials produced a rapid, highly sensitive and selective sensor for the detection of the target analyte in trace amounts. The fabricated composite nanoprobe was characterized and the detection condition was investigated. The probe was then applied to detect cefazolin in milk.

Cefazolin, formic acid, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99 %) and citric acid were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Japan). Ethanol, acetonitrile, NaOH and NH3 were from Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). Di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4⋅12H2O) was from Univar. Titanium dioxide powder (TiO2, 0.1−0.5 μm) was from Sigma-Aldrich. An RF-5310 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for fluorescence measurement. JEM-2010 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and JEM-5200 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL, Japan) were used to investigate the morphology of GQDs and nanocomposite probes. Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were investigated by ASAP 2460 (Micromeritics, USA).

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of 2.0 g citric acid at 250 °C. The obtained solution was dropped into 100 mL sodium hydroxide and then 1.50 mL of APTES were added into the mixture solution and stirring continued for 30 min. The synthesized GQDs were purified using a 12 kDa cut-off dialysis bag under stirring for 12 h.

To prepare the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes, 20 mg of cefazolin, the template, were mixed with 50 μL of APTES, the functional monomer, in 5.0 mL of deionized water under stirring at 30 °C for 1 h. The template molecule (cefazolin) formed hydrogen bonding with functional monomer during this step [26,27]. Then, 5.0 mL of synthesized GQDs and 5.0 mg of TiO2 were added to the mixture and stirring continued for 1 h. Subsequently, 200 μL of TEOS and 150 μL of NH3 were added into the solution and stirring continued for 3 h before the template molecules were removed with 10 mL of ethanol using sonication for 10 min. Washing was repeated three times until no template molecule remained in the nanocomposite probe. The synthesized GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes were dried at 60 °C and kept in desiccator until used. Non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was also fabricated without the cefazolin template. The synthesis of GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

To measure the ﬂuorescence intensities of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP and GQDs-TiO2-NIP probes. The nanoprobe solution was prepared by dispersion of 20 mg of GQDs-TiO2-MIP or GQDs-TiO2-NIP nanoprobes in 20 mL citrate-phosphate buffer at pH 6.0 and 300 μL of the dispersions were mixed with 100 μL of cefazolin standard or sample solution and incubated for 15 min. Measurements were recorded in the emission range from 400 to 680 nm (Excitation at 355 nm and slit width of 10 nm). The determination was performed at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample pretreatment and real sample analysis

ce:section.ce:para: HPLC analysis of cefazolin was performed using an Agilent 1100 HPLC system (Germany). The separation column was a Fortis C18 (5.0 μm, 15 cm ×4.6 mm). The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.05 % (v/v) formic acid (20:80 % v/v) at a constant flow rate of 0.80 mL min−1. Detection was performed at 254 nm. The sample injection volume and column temperature were 20 μL and 30 °C, respectively.

The FT-IR technique was used to investigate the functional groups of GQDs, TiO2 and a nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe. The GQDs showed an absorption peak at 1592 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretching of the carboxyl group (Fig. S1a). The broad absorption band at about 3000 to 3700 cm−1 was ascribed to the OH stretching and the peak at 2934 cm−1 corresponded to the C–H stretching vibration [29]. In the FT-IR spectrum of TiO2 (Fig. S1b), the absorption bands at 1380 and 1630 cm−1 were attributed to Ti-O and Ti−OH stretching [30], respectively. The broad absorption band of OH stretching vibration was present between 3000 and 3700 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum of nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP (Fig. S1c), the peaks at 778 and 1067 cm−1 corresponded to Si-O and Si-O-Si stretching vibrations, respectively. The presence of absorption peaks also generated by GQDs and TiO2 indicated that both GQDs and TiO2 were successfully composited with MIP.

The surface morphologies of GQDs and nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes were investigated using TEM and SEM. A TEM image of GQDs (Fig. 2 A) showed particles measuring about 9 nm, while the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes measured about 480 nm (Fig. 2B). The rough surface of the nanocomposite probes revealed in the SEM image was due to the formation of specific recognition cavities. Particle size distributions were presented in histograms of GQDs (Fig. 2C) and nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes ( Fig. 2D).

The fluorescence spectrum of a GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe containing the cefazolin template (Fig. S2a) showed that fluorescence intensity was low when cefazolin was bound with the nanocomposite probe. After template removal (Fig. S2b), fluorescence intensity was almost the same as that of the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-NIP probe (Fig. S2c). The fluorescence spectra indicated that template was completely removed and that recognition sites were available to rebind for the detection of cefazolin.

The quantum yields of GQDs and nanocomposite probes were calculated as described in a previous work [31]. The quantum yields of GQDs, GQDs-MIP and GQDs-TiO2-MIP were 0.66, 0.36 and 0.31, respectively (Rhodamine 6 G as the standard). These results implied that GQDs had good optical properties for sensing applications.

The specific surface areas of nanocomposite MIP and NIP probes were studied by the BET method. The surface areas of GQDs-TiO2-MIP and GQDs-TiO2-NIP probes were 24.0220 m2 g−1 and 18.6308 m2 g−1, respectively. The higher surface areas of nanocomposite MIP probes was due to the production of recognition cavities on the surface. These results also confirmed the successful fabrication of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes.

The detection condition using the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes was optimized to achieve the best sensitivity in the shortest detection time. The affecting parameters studied included incubation time, the pH of the buffer, the loading of TiO2 and the mole ratio of template to monomer and cross linker.

The incubation time required to bind cefazolin and the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was investigated from 2 to 30 min. Quenching efficiency (F0/F) increased with incubation time from 2 to 15 min and then remained constant ( Fig. 3 A). Thus, an incubation time of 15 min was chosen for the binding of cefazolin with recognition sites on the nanoprobe.

Since, the detection of cefazolin in sample solutions was carried out using a dispersion of nanocomposite probes in a buffer solution, the pH of buffer solution could affect the sensitivity of the detection. The effect of buffer pH was investigated between 4 and 7. The best sensitivity was achieved at pH 6 (Fig. 3B). In this condition, the stability of cefazolin is good. It has also been reported that the optimum stability of cefazolin in aqueous solutions is at pH 6 [4]. Thus, cefazolin in samples was detected using a dispersion of nanocomposite probes in a buffer at pH 6.0.

The amount of TiO2 used in the fabrication of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes was investigated from 25 mg to 100 mg. The results showed that 50 mg provided the best sensitivity ( Fig. 3C). The lower sensitivity obtained at 25 mg of TiO2 may have been due to the lower affinity between cefazolin and the nanocomposite probe. The lower sensitivity obtained with probes fabricated using 75 mg of TiO2 was due to an excess of TiO2 that disturbed the formation of recognition sites of the nanocomposite probes. Consequently, the nanocomposite probe was fabricated using 50 mg of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mole ratio of template (TP) to monomer (MN) and cross linker (CL)
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio of TP to MN and CL could affect the physical properties and number of binding sites of the fabricated GQDs-TiO2-MIP nanocomposite probes. Thus, the ratio was optimized. The highest sensitivity for cefazolin detection was obtained with a probe fabricated using a TP to MN mole ratio of 1:6 (Fig. 4 A). The sensitivity observed at the TP to MN mole ratio of 1:4 may have been lower because fewer binding sites were produced on the surface of the nanocomposite probe. The lower sensitivity at TP to MN mole ratios of 1:8 and 1:10 may have been caused by excess monomer that disturbed the formation of the MIP layer. The TP to CL ratio also affected sensitivity toward cefazolin detection. The TP to CL mole ratio of 1: 35 provided the best sensitivity (Fig. 4B). At a ratio of 1:30, sensitivity was lower since the MIP layer was too soft and at ratios of 1:40 and 1:45 the MIP layer was too dense which affected the specific binding sites. Consequently, the mole ratio of TP to MN to CL of 1:6:35 was chosen for the fabrication of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of different probes toward detection of cefazolin
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs-NIP, GQDs-TiO2-NIP, GQDs-MIP and GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes were evaluated for their sensitivity toward cefazolin in the optimum conditions. The sensitivity of each probe was 0.037 ± 0.011, 0.152 ± 0.023, 0.529 ± 0.019 and 0.702 ± 0.019 L μg−1, respectively (Fig. 5 ). GQDs-NIP exhibited the lowest sensitivity due to the absence of recognition sites and affinity material for cefazolin adsorption. Sensitivity increased when TiO2 was incorporated into the nanocomposite NIP probe because non-specific binding occurred between TiO2 and cefazolin. The sensitivity of the MIP probe was much better but the composite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe exhibited the highest sensitivity since the MIP contained specific recognition sites for cefazolin and affinity binding could occur between cefazolin and TiO2. Therefore, the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was used for the analysis of cefazolin.

The performances of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe were evaluated for the determination of cefazolin in the optimum condition. The intensity of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was significantly reduced by increasing concentrations of cefazolin (Fig. 7 A), while the intensity of the NIP was slightly reduced (Fig. 7B). The quenching of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was the result of binding between cefazolin and specific recognition sites, while the quenching of the NIP was due to non-specific adsorption of cefazolin on TiO2.

The quenching of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe by cefazolin was described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1+ Ksv[C],
where F0 and F were the intensity of the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe in the absence and presence of cefazolin, respectively. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant which is the slope of the linearity and [C] was the concentration of the quencher, cefazolin. The optosensor exhibited a good linearity from 0.10 to 10.0 μg L−1 (y = 0.701 ± 0.018)x + (0.944 ± 0.080)) with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996. The sensitivity of the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe for detection of cefazolin can be described by Ksv. A high value of Ksv indicates high quenching efficiency (sensitivity). A photograph (Fig. 7C) was taken to show the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe in the presence and absence of cefazolin under UV light. Light emission was strong in the absence of cefazolin and no light emission was observed in the presence of cefazolin. The LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 0.10 and 0.38 μg L−1, based on 3SD/slope and 10SD/slope, respectively.

The imprinting factor (IF), which is the ratio of Ksv of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP and GQDs-TiO2-NIP probes, was used to evaluate the specificity of the optosensor. The developed nanoprobe had a high imprinting factor of 10.6, which indicated that the recognition cavities on the probes were highly specific for cefazolin. The absorption spectrum of cefazolin did not overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe (Fig. S3), indicating that energy transfer was not possible [32]. Quenching was therefore caused by electron transfer from the nanocomposite probe to the cefazolin molecule.

The stability of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was evaluated using a dispersion of nanocomposite probes in buffer solution at pH 6. The results showed that fluorescence intensity did not change significantly within 4 h, and that the response at 4 h was better than 93 % of the original intensity (Fig. S4). In addition, the nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes were still stable after storage in a desiccator at 25 °C for more than 3 months.

The reproducibility of the fabrication of the GQDs-TiO2-MIP probe was also investigated. Six different lots of nanocomposite probes were fabricated at different times and used to detect cefazolin under the optimum conditions. The acceptable RSD of 2.1 % indicated that the reproducibility of the fabrication of the nanocomposite probe was good (Fig. S5).

The performance of the developed optosensor using nanocomposite GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes for cefazolin detection was compared with previously reported detection methods (Table 2 ). The developed optosensor provided wide linearity and a much lower LOD than other methods. The accuracy in terms of recovery and RSD were better than or comparable other methods. Therefore, the developed optosensor using GQDs-TiO2-MIP probes could be utilized as an alternative method for the measurement of cefazolin in milk. The developed optosensor was not only highly sensitive and selective but also cost-effective and simple to operate.

A nano-optosensor was developed for simple, rapid and cost-effective cefazolin detection. The optosensor comprised nanocomposite fluorescence probes composed of TiO2 and GQDs embedded in a MIP. The integration of the high affinity of TiO2, the good optical properties of GQDs and the high selectivity of MIP produced a rapid optosensor that detected trace cefazolin in complex samples. The recognition sites were highly specific for cefazolin with an imprinting factor of 10.6. Applied to detect cefazolin in milk, this optosensor obtained recoveries in the range of 85.0−97.4 %. The results obtained from this optosensor agreed well with HPLC technique. The developed method had a good accuracy and the developed optosensor could be modified to determine other compounds.

Nutnicha Chansud: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Opas bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Prince of Songkla University Grant number SCI6302085S, the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), the National Research Council of Thailand, Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. Nutnicha Chansud was supported by the Faculty of Science Research Fund, Prince of Songkla University, Contract no. 1-2563-02-001. Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne is also acknowledged for assistance with the English text of this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2020.113715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous quantification of fentanyl, sufentanil, cefazolin, doxapram and keto-doxapram in plasma using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Biomed. Chromatogr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.B. Flint, S. Bahmany, B.C.H. van der Nagel, B.C.P. Koch, 2018. Simultaneous quantification of fentanyl, sufentanil, cefazolin, doxapram and keto-doxapram in plasma using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, Biomed. Chromatogr. 32, e4290.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extraction and determination of cefazolin sodium and cefotaxime sodium in human urine with a weak ion exchange monolithic column
J. Liq. Chromatogr. Related Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhu, K. Row, Extraction and determination of cefazolin sodium and cefotaxime sodium in human urine with a weak ion exchange monolithic column, J. Liq. Chromatogr. Related Technol. 32 (2009) 1423-1433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a novel chemiluminescence method for the determination of cefazolin sodium in injectable powder and human urine based on a luminol-Cu(III) complex reaction in alkaline medium
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sun, J. Wang, T. Wang, Development of a novel chemiluminescence method for the determination of cefazolin sodium in injectable powder and human urine based on a luminol-Cu(III) complex reaction in alkaline medium, Luminescence. 28 (2013) 592-596.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies for the determination of cefazolin in plasma and microdialysis samples by short-end capillary zone electrophoresis
Electrophoresis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mayer, M. Petsch, E. Tschernko, M. Müller, Strategies for the determination of cefazolin in plasma and microdialysis samples by short-end capillary zone electrophoresis, Electrophoresis. 24 (2003) 1215-1220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative analysis of cefazolin sodium in lyophilized powder by infrared spectrophotometry: green, low cost, fast and effective
Spectrochim. Acta A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Rechelo, A. Kogawa, H. Salgado, Quantitative analysis of cefazolin sodium in lyophilized powder by infrared spectrophotometry: Green, low cost, fast and effective, Spectrochim. Acta A. 208 (2019) 157-161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric method for the determination of cefepime, cefazolin sodium and cefalothin sodium in pure and pharmaceutical dosage forms by using ninhydrin
Int. J. Pharm. Pharm. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.K. Papanna, J. Krishnegowda, Spectrophotometric method for the determination of cefepime, cefazolin sodium and cefalothin sodium in pure and pharmaceutical dosage forms by using ninhydrin, Int. J. Pharm. Pharm. Sci. 7 (2015) 194-199.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of total or free cefazolin and metronidazole in human plasma or interstitial fluid by HPLC-UV for pharmacokinetic studies in man
J. Chromatogr. B.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dorn, A. Kratzer, S. Schießer, F. Kees, H. Wrigge, P. Simon, Determination of total or free cefazolin and metronidazole in human plasma or interstitial fluid by HPLC-UV for pharmacokinetic studies in man, J. Chromatogr. B. 1118 (2019) 51-54.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A polypyrrole doped with fluorescent CdTe quantum dots and incorporated into molecularly imprinted silica for fluorometric determination of ampicillin
Microchim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Raksawong, P. Nurerk, K. Chullasat, P. Kanatharana, O. Bunkoed, 2019. A polypyrrole doped with fluorescent CdTe quantum dots and incorporated into molecularly imprinted silica for fluorometric determination of ampicillin, Microchim. Acta. 186, e338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective determination of copper ions in water samples based on the fluorescence quenching of thiol-capped CdTe quantum dots
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Nurerk, P. Kanatharana, O. Bunkoed, A selective determination of copper ions in water samples based on the fluorescence quenching of thiol-capped CdTe quantum dots, Luminescence. 31 (2015) 515-522.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CdSe quantum dots capped with a deep eutectic solvent as a fluorescent probe for copper (II) determination in various drinks
Microchim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sadeghi, A. Davami, 2020. CdSe quantum dots capped with a deep eutectic solvent as a fluorescent probe for copper (II) determination in various drinks, Microchim. Acta. 187, e147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercaptopropionic acid–capped CdTe quantum dots as fluorescence probe for the determination of salicylic acid in pharmaceutical products
Luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Bunkoed, P. Kanatharana, Mercaptopropionic acid–capped CdTe quantum dots as fluorescence probe for the determination of salicylic acid in pharmaceutical products, Luminescence. 30 (2015) 1083-1089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A nanocomposite probe of graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles embedded in a selective polymer for the enrichment and detection of ceftazidime
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Bunkoed, P. Raksawong, R. Chaowana, P. Nurerk, 2020. A nanocomposite probe of graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles embedded in a selective polymer for the enrichment and detection of ceftazidime, Talanta. 218, e121168.
